feedback.text: I would like to support the Digital Fairness Act initiative due to the following points: 1 – the presence of dark patterns concerning the termination of digital subscriptions. A few years ago, I observed in my previous job (SaaS) techniques for preventing terminations (prevention churn), such as: (A) Cacher the button “Cancel” (B) artificially Create steps not needed to unsubscribe. (C) Request to call the client service to unsubscribe. (D) Request to leave an audio message about the reasons for unsubscription. (E) Do believe that the subscription has been cancelled while it has been taken on a break. (F) Provide rebates aggressively so that the customer does not dissubscribe. = > All data (churn, Paused, coupons redemptions, drop out) was monitored on a daily basis and linked to managers’ bonuses. 2 – the presence of a paid loot box option in video games. The ‘loot box’ or ‘gatcha’ game system is, in my view, akin to gambling. The game encourages players to spend money on mini-lotteries to earn more or less rare digital collectors – the smallest ones with minimal chances of winning. This forces players to spend more. This phenomenon is aggravated by the possibility of reselling these items to other players for real money, leaving the door open to all abuses – including mafia drifts. 3 – the possibility for a video game publisher to ‘destroy’ video games. For more details, see ECI (2024) 000007 – Stop Destroying Videogames, which has so far collected more than 1.400.000 signatures. Today, video game publishers have, in my view, every power to disable access to a remote multiplayer game at the end of the game medium (Ubisoft for the game ‘The Crew’). Consumers who have paid for their games are thus deprived of their purchases without compensation or alternatives for consumers (such as private servers). I would like to respond in advance to the objections of video game publishers that, if imposed on them, this would make video games more expensive and risky. The price argument misses the point because (1) it is the same nuanced argument that consumer protection will cost consumers (with arguments like it would still be in cars without airbags...) and (2) how video game publishers manage their servers, their communities and the private data of users, it is ironic to consider that private servers may be a potential problem. 4 – unfair and arbitrary terms of payment providers such as Visa and MasterCard: In my view, in a democracy such as France or the European Union, it is not acceptable for private payment providers (and of US and quasi-monopolistic surplus) to act as an arbiter of good motives and deprive European consumers of their fundamental right to be able to purchase legal services or goods. I consider that payment providers should be neutral, as is the case for water, gas and electricity distributors. I have recently been victims of these abusive practices, both personally: —Visa pressured Steam (online video game distributor) to change its terms of use to include a clause prohibiting Steam from selling legitimate games that do not comply with Visa’s terms of use. As a result, Steam had to remove dozens of games from his platform. I would like to recall that these games were legal and I can no longer buy them. = > It is a dangerous slope that gives extraordinary and anti-democratic decision-making power to private companies.
